_comment: Worked examples embedded in the Semantic Mode clarifier prompt (A3). Two domains are included so the LLM sees that a 'good' clarifying question is not a statistical question; it is a question whose answer would change the modeling approach. The product launch example shows a non-clinical, non-financial decision; the pulmonary embolism example anchors to the original distribclin domain (Principle 4 calibration). Both examples are static reference data, not product data. They are inlined verbatim into the system prompt by lib/semantic/clarify.ts and never sent through any code path that consumes them as live LLM output.

examples.userQuery: Will our Q3 product launch hit 10k signups in the first month?

questions.question: What product segment is this? B2B SaaS, consumer mobile, hardware, or something else?
questions.why: Conversion rates and launch dynamics differ by 10x or more across segments; without knowing the segment the prediction is anchored on the wrong base rate.

questions.question: Are you counting signups including waitlist conversions, or only post-launch net-new users?
questions.why: Including waitlist makes the 10k target much easier to hit; the prediction needs the same definition the team will report on.

questions.question: What is your current pre-launch funnel volume (website visits, waitlist size, pre-orders)?
questions.why: Without an observed baseline the prediction is a pure prior; with the baseline we can anchor on your actual conversion behavior.
questions.defaultAnswer: Treat the question as 'we have a 5,000-person waitlist and 20k monthly site visitors' if you don't have the numbers handy.

examples.userQuery: What is the probability this 55-year-old with chest pain has a pulmonary embolism?

questions.question: Has a D-dimer been drawn, and if so what was the result relative to your lab's cutoff?
questions.why: D-dimer drives Bayesian update of the pre-test probability; whether the value is above or below cutoff swings the post-test probability dramatically.

questions.question: Are there major Wells-score risk factors present (recent surgery, active cancer, immobilization, prior VTE)?
questions.why: These factors shift the pre-test probability by tens of percentage points; modeling without them anchors the answer on a generic population mean.

questions.question: Is the decision you are trying to support 'should we order a CTPA?' or 'can we safely discharge?'
questions.why: The two decisions need different thresholds: rule-out wants very high sensitivity, discharge wants very high negative predictive value. The threshold flows from the decision, not the other way around.
